title: First trade deficit since 2017 opens ‘possible’ contraction
full_text:
Australia’s big banks have downgraded their economic growth forecasts, including the prospect of the first quarterly contraction since the Covid pandemic, after the country recorded its first trade deficit since 2017.
The Commonwealth Bank expects the Australian economy did not grow at all in the first three months of this year, leaving the annual rate around 2.1 per cent, down from 2.6 per cent.
The other three large banks also downgraded GDP forecasts ahead of official figures released on Wednesday, with ANZ predicting 0.5 per cent quarterly growth, and NAB 0.3 per cent.
Westpac also expects 0.3 per cent growth for the quarter but went further, saying the June quarter, when the Iran War started to bite, could see a “contraction” in economic activity – the “first quarterly decline since the (global financial crisis)” outside of Covid. Two consecutive quarterly contractions make a recession.
The bank said Tuesday’s figures showed the economy was already “slowing before the conflict in the Middle East” and before the triple rate hikes. Albanese government ministers had largely been blaming the war in the Middle East for a softer economy.
Westpac also said there had been a “softer than expected contribution” from government spending because of the end of the electricity rebates, leading to the “first quarterly fall in public consumption since Q3 2022”.
Commonwealth Bank’s economists said “this would indicate that growth in the Australian economy has returned to around its speed limit. From here, we are expecting (it) to slow.
“Three interest rate hikes, higher inflation, offshore uncertainty due to the Middle East conflict and the new downside risk from a soggy housing market are key catalysts for a slowdown.” ANZ said “momentum has waned, and we expect a step down in growth over the rest of the year”.
On Tuesday, Jim Chalmers said he would welcome any annual GDP growth rate “with a two in front of it”.
“We’ve got investment booming in the first quarter,” the Treasurer said. “We’ve got exports off quite substantially and … public demand making no contribution to growth in the first quarter. We know that already. We’ll see the rest of the numbers tomorrow.” The $5.2bn fall in net trade was driven by a rise in equipment imports for the construction of data centres. This fall is expected to shave 0.8 percentage points off GDP figures expected on Wednesday.
The deficit was driven by a 0.8 per cent growth in imports in the last quarter, Bureau of Statistics figures showed, driven by a 1.5 per cent rise in goods – particularly in automated data processing equipment, fuels and lubricants.
“ADP equipment imports reached historic highs, led by bulk imports of AI server racks amid continued data centre infrastructure investment in NSW and Victoria,” ABS head of international statistics Jonathon Khoo said.
“Crude oil and refined petroleum product prices rose significantly as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz lifted oil prices and tightened global supply.” On the other end of the equation, exports fell 1.2 per cent, driven by a fall in iron ore prices and depressed iron ore and coal exports.
Australia’s net international investment position included an $85bn fall in net foreign equity asset position, due to weaker foreign equity markets and a higher Australian dollar.
“Volatility in overseas equity markets and exchange rate movements due to the Middle East conflict reduced the value of Australian superannuation investment in overseas equity,” Mr Khoo said.
“This, combined with strong demand by overseas investors for Australian debt, drove Australia’s international investment position to its highest liability level since December 2023.” The current account balance fell $4.1bn to a $27.1bn deficit in seasonally adjusted terms, and the balance on goods and services fell $3.5bn to a $2.4bn deficit.
